textPlain: I used to live there.Offspring of welsh inmigrants are nicknamed "galenzos", they have a characteristic red curly hair and other subtle facial features (narrow lips, round nose tip). In average, better looking than non-welsh average. They are hard-working, calm, happy, serious, polite, reserved, good values. They've built beautiful things. They've spread their manners to the town and created a loving neighbourly atmosphere. (unlike the mapuche tribe people, who have produced the local criminal and ugly population)
I see someone else here also listens to Ken and Robin Talk About Stuff...

Us Britons have managed to cause some remarkably odd outcomes, way beyond the usual Empire bollocks and this is an absolute belter.Welsh is a Brythonic language, which is the most common class of languages spoken across what is now the UK(ish) that predates the Roman invasion in 55/54BC and 43 AD.  It's way older than English.  Other living examples include Scottish, Irish, Cornish (revived) and Bretton (off of France).  There are, of course, dialects and so on.I think it is absolutely delightful that a small part of Patagonia speaks Welsh.  In a world hell bent on painting itself beige this is a lovely thing.  Diversity is important in all walks of life.
Not mentioned in the article but you can drink good beer there.
Why does this entry not show up on https://news.ycombinator.com/from?site=wikipedia.org ?Edit: I guess it’s because of the mobile URL…?
FWIW, Scottish and Irish (and their sister Manx) are not Brythonic, they are in the other branch of extant Celtic languages, Goidelic/Gaelic.
it does, but on the second page, because it was originally posted 3 days ago.
